Intro: ^Looks like I'm... Jus imhime... time to... Justin...
^What? What are you talking about, did you have a concussion or something?
^The car started swerving, so I panicked. So what? Big deal, happens to the best of us. I didn't cry like a baby or nothing.
^But you were barely going 20km an hour...
^I left my fate in the hands of God, and he led me right where I needed to be. Praise his name.
^I started flooring it. Sadly, my foot got stuck on the pedal. Not much to be done about that.
^You shouldn't have floored it to begin with, but I guess you're right.
^Anyway, what took you so long? I've been waiting for quite a while.
^My car broke down on my way here, but it's alright. I fixed it using my <color=#FFFF00>Detective Vision.</color>
^Uh... Ok. I don't know what that means. It sounds cool though, I'm happy for you.
^Stop wasting my time, officer. Let's get on with the briefing.
^Alright... Walk with me, it's just over here.
Brief: ^What's the MO, chief? What's the beef, who's the perp? 10-17, over.
^Last night, the body of 27-year old Peter Grip was found lying here. The official cause of death is yet to be determined, but knowing him I'd say it came as no surprise.
^Why do you say that? Was he murdered, perhaps?
^No, no. Nothing like that. He was the town drunk! I've dealt with him before on many occassions when he's had a few too many at the bar.
^We tested his blood alcohol, and it was surprisingly high even for a guy like him. Seems like his liver gave up, kidneys shut down... And the rest is history.
^No witnesses have come forward to share any information either. We don't have much to go on, but I think it's pretty evident what happened.

c-0: ^As you said, somebody found the body. That someone must have phoned it in?

b: ^I crashed my car behind his shop.
^Then I take it that you've already introduced yourselves. I hope you apologized to him, I heard him shouting at you when you arrived...
^Don't you worry, we squashed the beef.
^He's the falafel guy. He has his shop right behind where you crashed your car.
^It's a wonder that you didn't drive it straight into his shop... You should probably apologize to him later.

c-0: ^Did you get an initial statement from him?
^I didn't feel like it, to be honest. Though I figured that you could take care of that.
^Peter was also a regular there, I'm pretty sure. You could ask him if he came by yesterday.

cont: ^Before you talk to Boss Man, you should take a moment to inspect the scene. I doubt you'll find anything useful, but go ahead. Knock yourself out.
^If you ever need a refresher on how your Detective Vision works, open up your Field Manual in the main menu.

b: ^C'mon, you barely scoured the scene. Go back and make sure that you didn't miss anything.
^Not that I care, but just in case.

Questions: ^Did you find anything of note, detective?
^Did you beat this poor drunk man to death and then rob him, officer?
^Don't be silly. What do you mean?
^You say there's nothing that indicates a murder, or even a robbery gone wrong, but after a brief inspection I can safely say that you're either dumb or full of shit.
^Detective, if you have any questions about what you've found...

c-0: ^His pockets were empty, or emptied, rather. Did you find anything on him?
^You don't find that odd? You usually carry something with you like a wallet, or something at least. Could he have been robbed?
^He dropped his wallet a lot, so that's nothing weird. I have found it myself quite a few times actually, and gave it back to him the next time I inevitably had to arrest him for disorderly conduct.
^And even if he had something worth robbing him for, I'd like to see it. Unless he won big at the lottery or something, I doubt it.
c-1: ^He has some pretty serious injuries. From a struggle perhaps? Leading to his death?
^Well, he was no stranger to brawling. They're nothing compared to the state I've seen him in before.
^See that bruise on his cheek? I did that last week after he resisted an arrest. Right in the kisser!
^Everything else is just an amalgamation of injuries he's racked up from different people over the years. I do agree that it looks very awful.
c-2: ^What do you make of this, officer?
^You hold the tin of snus up to the officer's face. He takes a long good whiff.
^Fyfan, bort med den där! Det luktar skit!
^Doesn't it smell weird, though? I recognize this smell from somewhere, or something.
^It all smells the same to me.

finish: ^Look, detective; I know that you think this is yet another murder-mystery that you have to solve, but there's really nothing to it. Just take Boss Man's statement and call it a day.
^If you still feel the need to investigate more thoroughly, you can always come back to me and I'll help point you in the right direction.

b: ^ You can start with getting Boss Man's statement, as we've already discussed.
^ Still not satisfied with Boss Man's statement, huh? You could check out the bar across the street. Maybe someone's heard or seen something.

^ You're still here? Go home, detective. We have everything we need. If you still intend to stay, go to the gas station and get yourself a coffee or something at least. Maybe you could call a tow truck...

GetLead: ^I need to solve this case immediately. And I need suspects to solve the case. I can't solve a case if I don't have a suspect. Please, officer! I need help! Anything!
^Jesus, alright, relax man... I'll humour you, ok?

b: ^ You could try to track down his wallet somehow. Assuming he was robbed, then whoever robbed him should have his wallet. You just gotta find out who. Easy.
^ I'm sure he died because he was an alcoholic. But, if I were to put myself into your shoes, that's not a satisfying deduction. Maybe there's an alternative explanation to his death?

^ I see you found his wallet, but it's not exactly worth robbing him for. He could have had something else entirely that was the reason for robbing him. Just retrace your steps and see if anything new comes up.

GetLead: ^Retrace your steps, detective. Maybe something has showed up that you missed. Talk to people, look around.
SearchTrash: ^This case proved harder than I thought. I haven't found any more clues.
^That's a shame, detective. Maybe you'll find some important clues if you go around rummaging through any trash you find, Ha-Ha! :)
^That's... not a bad idea. I love trash.
AboutBossMan: ^First off, he has the victim's wallet.
^Second, he claims to have found it before a purchase was made at his shop, made using the victim's card.
^Third, and most important, he was the one who found the body and called it in. It could have been a ruse to make himself look less suspicious.
^Hm, I don't quite buy it. Boss Man knew that Peter was dirt poor. Killing him for some pocket change is idiotic, to say the least.

b: ^It was a bluff. As a last stand, he claimed to have only found the body of Peter, which he believed to be unconscious, and took the wallet as some sort of "payback", before realizing he was actually dead and subsequently calling the police.

^However, if Boss Man also had knowledge of Peter's trisslott, then that changes things quite a lot. He would absolutely have a reason for killing him, and an even bigger reason for digging himself deeper into these lies.
^ I suppose so. I do have other suspects in mind, though...
^ But I don't have any other suspects... Can't we just take him away, and the case is closed?

AboutBossMan: ^Summan av kardemumman: Inget var förgiftat (?), men Boss Man mördade offret för att komma åt trisslotten, vilket är varför han hade plånboken.
AboutStoreClerk: ^First, him and the victim had been overheard arguing over something. This something was a trisslott. A winning one, at that.
^The store clerk felt slighted at this, insinuating that Peter didn't deserve to win. That escalated into a brawl between the two, and Peter left the store shortly after.

^Second, there is a witness that can attest to the store clerk not being at the store around the time of the victim's death, around 5 minutes or so. Though not definitive proof, there is reasonable doubt.
^Hardly enough time to kill a person with only 5 minutes to spare.
^Not at all. However, considering that he may have been poisoned brings me to my third and final point: He laced Peter's snus with kylvätska, and he had been slowly poisoned throughout the night.
^When he surmised that enough time had passed that Peter should have died, he left his post and went to look for him.
^Normally he would have had an airtight alibi, since no one would question that he left the store. Sadly for him, there was a customer there that noticed that he was missing for those few minutes.
^Summan av kardemumman: Snusen var förgiftad med kylarvätska, för att komma åt trisslotten.
AboutBartender: ^First, he lied about his alibi. He claims that Peter and two other patreons were all together at his bar last night until closing.
^However, these other two patreons contradict this alibi. In reality, they both left together some hours before closing, leaving only the bartender alone with Peter.
^Second, during this time, he had ample opportunity to kill him.
^How so? He was found dead right here. He would have killed him at the bar if that was the case.
^He poisoned him. The bar suffers from a rat infestation, and the bartender has rat poison strewn about all over the place.
^And it seems to have found its way into his beer somehow.

^Third, there's the issue of the trisslott. I talked with two of Peter's aquaintances, and they both attest to him having won, big time. The bartender would also have known about it, but conviniently neglected to mention it.
^To sum up: When the bartender found out about the winning trisslott and the other patreons left him alone with Peter, he saw his opportunity.
^Summan av kardemumman: Ölen var förgiftad med råttgift, för att komma åt trisslotten.

murder_scene: ^The bedroom. This is where it happened. Now to look for clues.
^Use the cane to reach under the bed

c-0: ^The bed was low to the ground, but not so low something might not roll underneath. It was still neatly made.
^I lifted back the bedcover. The duvet underneath was crumpled.
c-1: ^Careful not to disturb anything beneath, I removed the cover entirely. The duvet below was rumpled.
^Not the work of the maid, who was conscientious to a point. Clearly this had been thrown on in a hurry.
c-2: ^I pulled back the duvet. Beneath it was a sheet, sticky with blood.
^Either the body had been moved here before being dragged to the floor - or this is was where the murder had taken place.
c-3: ^Carefully, I pulled the bedsheets back into place, trying to make it seem undisturbed.
c-4: ^I pushed the bed with spread fingers. It creaked a little, but not so much as to be obnoxious.
c-5: ^Lying down, I peered under the bed, but could make nothing out.
c-6: ^I took a step back from the bed and looked around.

c-1: ^I peered under the bed. Something glinted back at me.

c-0: ^I fished with one arm under the bed, but whatever it was, it had been kicked far enough back that I couldn't get my fingers on it.
c-2: ^I stood up once more, and brushed my coat down.
^Positioning the cane above the carpet, I gave the glinting thing a sharp tap. It slid out from the under the foot of the bed.

c-0: ^Satisfied, I stood up, and saw I had knocked free a bloodied knife.
c-1: ^Moving the cane aside, I looked under the bed once more, but there was nothing more there.

c-3: ^Careful not to touch the handle, I lifted the blade from the carpet.
c-4: ^The blood was dry enough. Dry enough to show up partial prints on the hilt!
c-5: ^I turned my attention to the desk. A lamp sat in one corner, a neat, empty in-tray in the other. There was nothing else out.
^Leaning against the desk was a wooden cane.

c-0: ^I picked up the wooden cane. It was heavy, and unmarked.
c-2: ^I regarded the in-tray, but there was nothing to be seen. Either the victim's papers were taken, or his line of work had seriously dried up. Or the in-tray was all for show.
c-4: ^I took a step away from the desk once more.

c-6: ^I was still holding the cane: I gave it an experimental swoosh. It was heavy indeed, though not heavy enough to be used as a bludgeon.
^But it might have been useful in self-defence. Why hadn't the victim reached for it? Knocked it over?
c-7: ^I went over to the window and peered out. A dismal view of the little brook that ran down beside the house.

b: ^Through the steamed glass I couldn't see the brook.

c-0: ^I watched the little stream rush past for a while. The house probably had damp but otherwise, it told me nothing.
c-1: ^The glass in the window was greasy. No one had cleaned it in a while, inside or out.
c-2: ^A cold day outside. Natural my breath should steam.
c-3: ^I breathed gently on the glass once more.

b: ^I looked away from the dreary glass.
^ The steam from my breath faded.

c-4: ^I leant back from the glass. My breath had steamed up the pane a little.

b: ^ A little light spilled under the bed.
^ The light gleamed on the polished tabletop.

compare_prints: ^Compare the prints on the knife and the window

c-0: ^Holding the bloodied knife near the window, I breathed to bring out the prints once more, and compared them as best I could.
^Hardly scientific, but they seemed very similar - very similiar indeed.

s0: ^But I could see a few fingerprints, as though someone hadpressed their palm against it.
s1: ^The fingerprints were quite clear and well-formed.

seen_light: ^ Move the light to the bed
^ Move the light back to the desk

c-1: ^I moved the light over to the bloodstain and peered closely at it. It had soaked deeply into the fibres of the cotton sheet.
^There was no doubt about it. This was where the blow had been struck.
c-2: ^I moved the light back to the desk, setting it down where it had originally been.
c-3: ^I picked the light up and set it down on the floor.

joe_in_hall: ^My police contact, Joe, was waiting in the hall. 'So?' he demanded. 'Did you find anything interesting?'

s: ^'They match a set of prints on the window, too.'
^'The body was moved to the bed at some point
^'The victim was murdered in bed, and then the body was moved to the floor.'

c-1: ^'Good going!' Joe replied with a grin. 'We thought the murderer had gotten rid of it. I'll bag that for you now.'
c-2: ^'Hrm. Not very complete. It'll be hard to get a match from these.'
c-3: ^'Anyone could have touched the window,' Joe replied thoughtfully. 'But if they're more complete, they should help us get a decent match!'
c-4: ^,' I told him. 'And then moved back to the floor.'

s: ^'Perhaps to get something from the floor?'

c-1: ^'You wouldn't move a whole body for that.'
c-2: ^'It's just speculation at this point,' Joe remarks.

s: ^'Perhaps the murderer wanted to mislead us.'
^'Perhaps the murderer hoped to clean up the scene.'
^'They wanted us to think the victim was awake
^'They wanted us to think there was some kind of struggle

c-0: ^, I replied thoughtfully. 'That they were meeting their attacker, rather than being stabbed in their sleep.'
c-1: ^,' I replied. 'That the victim wasn't simply stabbed in their sleep.'
g-0: ^'But if they were killed in bed, that's most likely what happened. Stabbed, while sleeping.'

c-6: ^'All right. It's a start,' Joe replied.

b: ^ 'I'll get those prints from the window now.'
^ 'I'll run those prints as best I can.'
